explicit_text: I first learned about the Veterans History Project through a cousin who had participated in it about a year before telling me. He encouraged me to get involved, especially after I mentioned I had been on the Honor Flight. It was a great opportunity to share my experiences and contribute to the Smithsonian's collection of veteran stories. I'm glad I took part and could share my story through this project.

explicit_text: I had the chance to go on the Honor Flight, which was something my cousin asked me about when he mentioned the Veterans History Project. It was a meaningful experience that connected me with other veterans and honored our service. The Honor Flight helped me reflect on my time in the Army and the sacrifices made during the war. It was a significant part of my post-service life.

explicit_text: I didn't know much about the Smithsonian Institution until my cousin told me that interviews from the Veterans History Project were going into their collection. It was exciting to realize that my story would be preserved there alongside many others. The Smithsonian plays an important role in keeping the history of veterans alive for future generations. Being part of that was an honor.

explicit_text: I entered the service on March 19, 1945, while I was living at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor. This was near the end of World War II, and the war in Europe was winding down. It was a time filled with anticipation and uncertainty, as we all knew the conflict was nearing its conclusion but still had important battles ahead. That date marked the beginning of my military journey.

That date marked the beginning of my military journey.

explicit_text: Before joining the Army, I was a student at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor, studying Pre-Med. I had completed almost my first year thanks to their three-quarter system before being drafted. Being at the university was a formative time, but like many young men then, I was always aware that I might be called to serve. It was a bittersweet transition from college life to military duty.

explicit_text: Ann Arbor was where I lived while attending the University of Michigan before I was drafted. It was a college town filled with students and a sense of normalcy, even as the war raged on. Living there made me realize how different life would become once I entered the Army. Ann Arbor was the last place I called home before my military service began.

explicit_text: I attended Lake View High School in Chicago, at Ashland and Irving, and graduated in June before heading to college. My father wanted me to get as much education as possible, so I started college right away. Lake View was where my early education took place, shaping my readiness for both college and eventually the Army. It was the foundation of my academic and personal development.

explicit_text: Chicago was my hometown where I attended high school and had family, though by the time I was drafted, my family had moved to Detroit. I had connections there but no longer lived in the city. Chicago represented my roots and early life before the war changed everything. It was also a place I thought about during my service, especially when I was away at training camps.

explicit_text: Detroit became my home after my father was transferred there for work, and that's where I was inducted into the United States Army. My mother worked at the main Ration Board downtown during the war, which was an important job for the home front. Detroit was the city I returned to on furloughs and where my family was based during my service. It was a key place in my life during those years.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Army on March 19, 1945, after being drafted in Detroit. My time in the Army was mostly spent at Camp Robinson in Little Rock, Arkansas, where I was part of the Infantry Replacement Training Center. I was promoted to Corporal and served as a trainer for new recruits. The Army changed my life completely, from college student to soldier, and I experienced firsthand the realities of military service during World War II.

explicit_text: After being inducted in Detroit, I was sent to Fort Sheridan for initial processing before going on to Camp Robinson for Basic Training. Fort Sheridan was a stepping stone in my Army journey, a place where many soldiers passed through before heading to their assignments. It was close enough that I felt like I was still near home, but it marked the start of my military life.

explicit_text: Camp Robinson in Little Rock, Arkansas, was where I did my Basic Training and stayed on as part of the training cadre. It was an Infantry Replacement Training Center, not a permanent fort, but a busy place preparing soldiers for combat. I was promoted to Corporal there and helped train new recruits, which was a good break from frontline duty. Little Rock was the nearest city, and I sometimes got passes to visit it during my time at the camp.

It wasn't a big city, but it was nice, and I occasionally got passes to go there from the camp. Little Rock was a place where I could get a break from the rigors of training and experience some civilian life during the war. It was part of my Army experience in 1945.

explicit_text: At Camp Robinson, I was part of the Infantry Replacement Training Center, where soldiers were trained to replace combat losses. I was selected to be part of the cadre, serving as a trainer and helping new recruits learn the skills they needed. This role kept me from going overseas to fight, which was a relief but also made me feel a bit like a pariah. The IRTC was a critical part of the Army's effort to keep the infantry supplied with trained men during the war.

explicit_text: Harry S. Truman was President when the atomic bomb was dropped, which abruptly ended the war and changed my military plans. I was slated for promotion to Sergeant, but after the bomb was dropped, the war ended, and those plans were put on hold indefinitely. Truman's decision to use the bomb meant that I never had to go into combat as part of Operation Coronet. It was a pivotal moment that affected many soldiers' futures, including mine.
implicit_text: The President at the time was in office when the atomic bomb was dropped, which abruptly ended the war and changed my military plans. I was slated for promotion to Sergeant, but after the bomb was dropped, the war ended, and those plans were put on hold indefinitely. His decision to use the bomb meant that I never had to go into combat as part of the planned invasion of Japan. It was a pivotal moment that affected many soldiers' futures, including mine.

explicit_text: While at Camp Robinson, I was offered the chance to interview for Officer Candidate School because some officers appreciated my literacy and work ethic. I was interested and told my mother about it, but that ended the conversation, and I didn't pursue it further. Looking back, it might not have been a bad idea to become an officer, but at the time, I was ready to finish my service and return to civilian life. OCS was an opportunity that passed me by during my Army career.

explicit_text: Pearl Harbor was a defining event that shaped the feelings of many of us before we entered the service. Hearing about the attack made us feel like slackers if we weren't in uniform, and it motivated me and others to want to serve. It was part of the backdrop to my decision to join the Army and the general wartime atmosphere. Pearl Harbor's impact was still very much alive in our minds during my training and service.

explicit_text: The Bataan Death March was one of the tragic events that weighed on us as we prepared for service during World War II. It was part of the news and stories that made us feel the urgency and seriousness of the war. Knowing about such events made me and my peers eager to contribute and not just stand by. The Bataan Death March symbolized the harsh realities of the conflict we were about to face.

That march symbolized the harsh realities of the conflict we were about to face.

explicit_text: VE Day in early May 1945 marked the end of the war in Europe and brought a sudden halt to our training for the European theater. After VE Day, everything stopped at Camp Robinson, and we weren't training for Europe anymore. Instead, we prepared for the invasion of Japan, Operation Coronet, which was ultimately canceled after the atomic bomb was dropped. VE Day was a moment of relief but also uncertainty about what came next.
implicit_text: Victory in Europe Day in early May 1945 marked the end of the war in Europe and brought a sudden halt to our training for the European theater. After that day, everything stopped at Camp Robinson, and we weren't training for Europe anymore. Instead, we prepared for the invasion of Japan, which was ultimately canceled after the atomic bomb was dropped. That day was a moment of relief but also uncertainty about what came next.

explicit_text: Operation Coronet was the planned invasion of the Japanese home islands that we were preparing for after VE Day. I was part of the half a million troops being readied to go over there, and we were told one in three of us wouldn't come back. The dropping of the atomic bomb ended the need for that operation, sparing many lives, including mine. Operation Coronet was a looming mission that shaped the final months of my service.

explicit_text: The Battle of the Bulge was one of the major battles of World War II that we heard about while training and waiting to be deployed. It was part of the context that made us understand the severity of the war and the challenges soldiers faced. Although I didn't fight in that battle myself, it was often discussed among us as a reminder of what was at stake. The battle symbolized the intense fighting that characterized the war in Europe.

explicit_text: During my time in the Army, I earned the Expert Infantryman Badge, which recognized my proficiency and skills as an infantryman. It was a mark of honor that showed I had met the Army's standards in various infantry tasks. Earning this badge was a source of pride, especially since I was also involved in training others at Camp Robinson. It reflected my dedication and competence in the infantry role.

explicit_text: The M1 Garand was the standard rifle we used during training at Camp Robinson and was central to our infantry instruction. As a trainer, I helped new recruits learn to handle and maintain the M1 Garand, which was crucial for their effectiveness in combat. The rifle was a symbol of the infantryman's role and a key part of our preparation for battle. It represented the practical side of soldiering during World War II.
